message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:p>
                  The COVID-19 pandemic has highlighted limitations in case-based surveillance due to inconsistent testing and reporting. Wastewater-based epidemiology (WBE) has emerged as a complementary surveillance approach for tracking SARS-CoV-2 transmission, capturing both symptomatic and asymptomatic infections. The aim of this study is to evaluate the effectiveness of WBE in estimating the effective reproduction number (
                  <jats:italic>R</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>
                    <jats:italic>t</jats:italic>
                  </jats:sub>
                  ) of SARS-CoV-2 in Georgia, USA. We used a Generalised Linear Mixed Model to analyse viral concentration data from multiple wastewater treatment plants (WWTPs) collected between June 1, 2022 and December 15, 2022. After controlling for flow rates and site-level heterogeneity, model residuals were transformed into a non-negative incidence-like series used to estimate wastewater-based
                  <jats:italic>R</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>
                    <jats:italic>t</jats:italic>
                  </jats:sub>
                  . Wastewater-based
                  <jats:italic>R</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>
                    <jats:italic>t</jats:italic>
                  </jats:sub>
                  was compared with case-based
                  <jats:italic>R</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>
                    <jats:italic>t</jats:italic>
                  </jats:sub>
                  estimates using Spearman correlation. The two
                  <jats:italic>R</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>
                    <jats:italic>t</jats:italic>
                  </jats:sub>
                  estimates showed concordant temporal patterns across most sites, with stronger correlations in areas with higher case counts (Spearman correlations ranging from 0.39 to 0.84,
                  <jats:italic>p</jats:italic>
                  < 0.001). Wastewater-based
                  <jats:italic>R</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>
                    <jats:italic>t</jats:italic>
                  </jats:sub>
                  tracked increases and decreases in transmission over similar time scales as case-based estimates, while exhibiting reduced sensitivity to short-term changes in clinical testing and reporting behaviour. These findings suggest that WBE can support estimation of transmission trends and complement traditional case-based surveillance for public health monitoring.
                </jats:p>
title: Comparing Wastewater-Based and Case-Based R t Estimates of SARS-CoV-2 Transmission in Georgia, USA Using Generalised Linear Mixed Models
